pattern: Precomputing a frequency map of the smaller array lets each count query scan only the larger array once, while updates touch a single frequency bucket.
hints: A pair count for a target equals, for every value v in one array, how many elements equal target minus v in the other array.
Maintain a frequency map of one array so a query iterates the other array and sums looked-up counts in linear time.
Pick the array that gets mutated (nums2) to iterate during count, and keep a frequency map of the static array (nums1); add adjusts nums2 directly without rebuilding anything.

edge_inputs.description: statement example: counts before and after additions to nums2
single-element arrays; count a matching and a non-matching total
no pair reaches the requested total
duplicates in both arrays mean a single total is hit many times; count multiplicities, not distinct pairs
an add changes which totals are reachable; the same query before and after must differ
repeated adds to the same index accumulate
large values where the only matching pair uses the big nums1 element

stress.description: randomised mix of add and count over moderate arrays drawn from a small value domain so totals are frequently hit
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n1 = rng.randint(1, 40)
    n2 = rng.randint(1, 60)
    nums1 = [rng.randint(1, 30) for _ in range(n1)]
    nums2 = [rng.randint(1, 30) for _ in range(n2)]
    ops = ['FindSumPairs']
    args = [[nums1[:], nums2[:]]]
    for _ in range(size):
        if rng.random() < 0.4:
            idx = rng.randint(0, n2 - 1)
            val = rng.randint(1, 20)
            ops.append('add')
            args.append([idx, val])
        else:
            tot = rng.randint(2, 80)
            ops.append('count')
            args.append([tot])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: small value domain keeps many counts non-zero, exercising both operations
